coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated hierarchical Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structure for outstanding supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Guijuan
Xu, Xiuru
Liu, Junxue
Du, Kun
Du, Jiao
Zhang, Shuo
An, Changhua
Zhang, Jun
Wang, Zhaojie
coredata.dc:description: Ni(OH)2, as a promising supercapacitor electrode material, has attracted numerous research attention to design well-organized structures and hybrid materials. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated hierarchical Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structure (similar to a dessert of dragon eye pastry in Sichuan, China) was successfully fabricated by a facile and cost-effective hydrothermal method. The CQDs induces a great enhancement in electrochemical energy storage. The hybrid exhibits a specific capacitance of 2900Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 and good rate capability. Such remarkable electrochemical properties can be primarily ascribed to the uniform structure of hierarchical Ni(OH)2 and the significantly enhanced utilization of CQDs.

Carbon quantum dots
Hierarchical
Nanocomposites
Energy storage and conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) FE-SEM of pure Ni(OH)2, (c) TEM of the CQDs and (d) FE-SEM of CQDs /Ni(OH)2.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) TGA curve of CQDs /Ni(OH)2 composites.
CV curves of (a) Ni(OH)2 and (c)CQDs /Ni(OH)2, GCD curves of (b) Ni(OH)2 and (d) CQDs /Ni(OH)2 and comparison of (e) specific capacitance and (f) Nyquist plot of the Ni(OH)2 and the CQDs /Ni(OH)2.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated hierarchical Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structure for outstanding supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, China University of Petroleum, Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, China University of Petroleum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Material, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Advanced Material, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum, Qingdao, Shandong 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ni(OH)2, as a promising supercapacitor electrode material, has attracted numerous research attention to design well-organized structures and hybrid materials. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated hierarchical Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structure (similar to a dessert of dragon eye pastry in Sichuan, China) was successfully fabricated by a facile and cost-effective hydrothermal method. The CQDs induces a great enhancement in electrochemical energy storage. The hybrid exhibits a specific capacitance of 2900Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 and good rate capability. Such remarkable electrochemical properties can be primarily ascribed to the uniform structure of hierarchical Ni(OH)2 and the significantly enhanced utilization of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: With growing concerns regarding to plug-in electrical vehicles and various electronic devices, studies are increasingly focusing on energy storage and conversion from renewable energy sources [1,2]. In many application areas, supercapacitors have attracted significant attention because of their long cycle life, fast charging-discharging rate, high power density and environmental benignity [3,4]. Currently, a major challenge of conventional electric double-layer capacitors is to boost the energy density comparing with larger capacitance of pseudocapacitors based on the fast and reversible faradic redox reaction of electroactive materials such as transition metal oxides and conducting polymers [5,6].

Ni(OH)2 has been recognized as a promising electrode materials due to the low cost and high theoretical specific capacitance. However, the poor electrical conductivity and large volume change, commonly occurring during the charge/discharge process, greatly restrict its practical application. In attempts to improve its electrochemical performance, Ni(OH)2 nanostructures have been studied [4,7]. Particularly, three-dimensional (3D) architecture has attracted much interest because of the distinctive structural features and intriguing properties [8,9]. In comparison to low-dimensional materials, 3D structures can be honed to improve electronic and ionic conductivity and to increase capacitive surface areas. In addition to structure, introducing high-surface-area conductive carbonaceous materials to prepare composites is an effective and promising approach. Therefore, in order to further improve the electrochemical properties of Ni(OH)2, some carbon materials such as nanotubes and graphene have been utilized to fabricate the composite materials [9–11]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely investigated in many fields because of their unique properties of pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects. However, there are few reports about CQDs being undertaken as electrode materials for applications in supercapacitors. According to the previous reports, CQDs can act as superior conductive agents to enhance conductivity when they are combined with other materials so as to hold great promise for potential applications in energy storage field [12,13].

Considering the advantages of Ni(OH)2 and CQDs, the excellent electrochemical performance of the as-fabricated CQDs/Ni(OH)2 composites in our work is expected. First, hierarchical Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structure was successfully fabricated by a fast, facile and cost-effective hydrothermal method. Electrochemically anodic oxidation of ethanol was used to prepare CQDs of ～3nm before being decorated on Ni(OH)2. It demonstrates excellent electrochemical performance with remarkable specific capacitance, and high rate properties making CQDs/Ni(OH)2 promising for novel high-performance electrode materials.

Preparation of Ni(OH)2 and CQDs/Ni(OH)2 hybrid. In a typical experiment, 200mg of Ni(Ac)2·4H2O were dissolved in 23mL of deionized water and 1mL of glycerol with ultrasonication [14]. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave of 50mL cubage and heated at 200℃ for 1.5h. Hierarchical Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structures were obtained after washing with deionized water and ethanol for several times. 30mg of dried Ni(OH)2 powder was dispersed in 10mL of 4mg/mL CQDs before stirring for 30min at room temperature. The yellow green precipitate of CQDs/Ni(OH)2 was washed and centrifuged for three times and finally dried at 60℃ over night.

The surface morphology and size of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100UHR). The crystalline phases of the samples were analyzed by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, The Netherlands) on a Philips X′Pert diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15418nm). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was employed to evaluate the weight loss of the samples in a flow of air under a heating rate of 5℃min−1 through thermal analyzer (Perkin-Elmer PYRIS 1).

Electrodes used were prepared by pressing the slurry of electroactive material, carbon black, and poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (80:15:5 in weight) onto the nickel foam current collector (10mm*10mm) and dried at 100℃ for 12h. The electrochemical performance of the samples was studied in a three-electrode test system by using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. A platinum plate and a Hg/HgO electrode were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. 2M KOH aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

The SEM image in Fig. 1a shows that the pristine Ni(OH)2 with typical size from 1 to 3µm has the shape of a hierarchical ring with plenty lamellar structure stacked together. The enlarged SEM image (Fig. 1b) shows that each Ni(OH)2 nanoplate is ～20nm in thickness. These nanoplates with perfectly flat surface interconnect with each other to form big ordered aggregations similar to a dessert of dragon eye pastry in Sichuan, China (inset of Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, the average size of the CQDs is ～3nm. As we know, there would be elements O in CQDs by this method, which makes it feasible to be incorporated into other compounds [15]. It retains a similar hierarchical ring shape after heterostructured Ni(OH)2 decorated with CQDs (Fig. 1d)..

There is a uniform distribution of Ni, O, and C elements and weaken intensity in the center (as shown in Fig. S1). The structures of both pristine Ni(OH)2 and CQDs/Ni(OH)2 were characterized by XRD, as shown in Fig. 2a. All of the reflections in the XRD pattern can be indexed to β-Ni(OH)2 (JCPDS 14-0117). No characteristic peaks can be detected after being decorated with CQDs. As observed from the difference of weight loss in TGA analysis (Fig. 2b), the mass fraction of the CQDs in the composite is about 1.5%..

Fig. 3a and c show the CV plots of the Ni(OH)2 and CQDs/Ni(OH)2 electrodes at various scan rates in a potential range of 0–0.7V (vs. Hg/HgO). The obvious redox peaks in all of the CV curves indicate the capacitance primarily originates from the reversible conversion between Ni (II) and Ni (III). It should be noted that a significant positive shift of oxidation peaks and a negative shift of reduction peaks can be observed with an increase of scan rates, which is mainly due to the resistance of the electrode. However, it is different from CQDs/Ni(OH)2 composite electrode, which may be ascribed to the strong chemical interaction between Ni(OH)2 and residual oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs, or van der Waals interactions between Ni(OH)2 and CQDs. Surprisingly, with a 20-fold increase in the scan rate from 5 to 100mVs−1, the oxidation and reduction peak of the CQDs/Ni(OH)2 electrode shift less, suggesting the lower resistance of the electrode and the faster electronic and ionic transport rate..

To further understand the rate capability of the electrodes, the GCD measurements at various current densities ranging from 1 to 20Ag−1 were performed, as shown in Fig. 3b and d. The specific capacitances of CQDs/Ni(OH)2 electrode were calculated as 2900, 2695, 2360, 2051, 1557 and 1030Fg−1 at current densities of 1, 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20Ag−1, respectively (Fig. 3e). These values are much higher than that of the pristine Ni(OH)2 electrodes (362Fg−1 at 20Ag−1) and many previous reported results, e.g. Ni(OH)2/MWCNT hybrid paper [16]. The decrease in capacitance with higher current density is due to significant barrier to penetration and diffusion of electrolyte.

The Nyquist plots of the two electrodes obtained using AC EIS in the frequency range of 100–0.01Hz are shown in Fig. 3f. The CQDs/Ni(OH)2 hybrid electrode shows a better vertical line in the low frequency region than that of pristine Ni(OH)2, indicating a better capacitive behavior. In the high frequency region, the semicircle originates from the resistance between the electrode and the current collector, and among the electrode materials. The contact resistance of the x-axis intercept is an important factor in determining the power performance [17]. The contact resistance of the CQDs/Ni(OH)2 is smaller than pristine Ni(OH)2 and it means that the power performance of the former is better than the latter.

In summary, carbon quantum dots decorated hierarchical Ni(OH)2 has been successfully fabricated and studied for the electrode of supercapacitor. The specific capacitance of the hybrid material reaches 2900Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 and the capacitance still remains at 2051Fg−1 at high current density of 10Ag−1. The enhanced electrochemical performance is mainly attributed to the strong interaction between different components, the lower resistance of the electrode and the faster electronic and ionic transport rate. These results demonstrate a promising approach in development and realization of high-performance CQDs-based hybrid electrode materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51402362 and 21471160), Shandong Natural Science Foundation (ZR2014EMQ012), Qingdao Science and Technology Program for Youth (14-2-4–34-jch) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (16CX05016A and 16CX05014A).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.09.126.
